walkthrough.title: Prepare the Worker Bag
walkthrough.text: Let's get Keyflower set up. First, find all the wooden workers, called keyples. There are 40 blue, 40 red, and 40 yellow workers. Place all 120 of these into the black cloth bag and give it a good shake. The 20 green workers stay out of the bag for now. Place them in a visible supply at the side of the playing surface where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Sort the 120 octagonal wooden resource counters into four groups by type. You should have 48 gold (yellow), 24 iron (black), 24 stone (grey), and 24 wood (brown). Place each group in a separate pile at the side of the playing surface. This is your general supply that everyone will draw from throughout the game. Quick note: gold is special. It can substitute for any other resource at any time, including during scoring.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Skill Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 48 skill tiles. There are three types: 16 anvils, 16 pick axes, and 16 saws. Place them all face down in a single pile at the side of the playing surface. Players will draw from this pile during the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Screens
walkthrough.text: Each player receives a home screen. These are the folding screens with chimney pieces. Carefully construct each screen by using the separate chimney pieces to lock the walls and roofs into place. Your screen hides your workers, skill tiles, and any other secret information from other players.

walkthrough.title: Draw Starting Workers
walkthrough.text: Each player secretly draws eight workers at random from the cloth bag. Do not reveal the colors to other players. Place these workers behind your screen. You will likely have a mix of blue, red, and yellow workers. The colors you draw will heavily influence your early strategy, because all workers used on or around a single tile must be the same color.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Hexagonal Tiles
walkthrough.text: Sort the 64 large hexagonal tiles by type. You will see identifying icons on each. There are 6 home tiles, 6 boat tiles, 4 turn order tiles, 12 spring tiles, 12 summer tiles, 12 autumn tiles, and 12 winter tiles. Each season's tiles are marked with a distinct season symbol. Keep each group in a separate pile. You can use the included zip lock bags for storage.

walkthrough.title: Deal Home Tiles
walkthrough.text: Give each player one home tile at random. Place the home tile face up in front of your screen, within reach of other players. Home tiles are numbered. The player who received the lowest numbered home tile takes the purple start player worker. Any unused home tiles go back in the box.

walkthrough.title: Place the Boat Tiles
walkthrough.text: The number of boat tiles equals the number of players. Each boat tile is marked to indicate which player counts it is used for. Place the appropriate boat tiles in a row at the edge of the playing surface, with the side indicating the number of players face up, oriented toward the center of the table. Take a moment to look at the reverse side of each boat tile, which shows the scoring opportunity available in winter. Then place the indicated number of workers from the bag and skill tiles next to or on top of each boat tile, as shown on the tile.

walkthrough.title: Place Turn Order Tiles
walkthrough.text: The number and side of the turn order tiles depends on the player count. In a 2-player game you use 1, in a 3-player game you use 2, in a 4-player game you use 3, and in a 5 or 6-player game you use 4. Each tile is marked to indicate whether it is used and which side to show. Place them in a row next to the boat tiles, but closer to the center of the playing surface, with the correct side face up and oriented toward the center.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Spring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place the spring tiles in the center of the playing surface with the spring icon face up. The number of spring tiles depends on player count: 6 for 2 players, 7 for 3 players, 8 for 4 players, 9 for 5 players, or 10 for 6 players. Arrange them so each player can identify with one side of each tile for bidding. Any surplus spring tiles go back in the box.

walkthrough.title: Deal Winter Tiles
walkthrough.text: Each player receives face-down winter tiles. In 2, 3, or 4-player games each player gets 3 winter tiles. In 5 or 6-player games each player gets 2. You may peek at the reverse (scoring) side of your winter tiles, but do not show them to other players. These tiles will determine the end-game scoring opportunities. Any surplus winter tiles go back in the box.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside Summer and Autumn Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place all 12 summer tiles and all 12 autumn tiles in two separate face-down piles. These will be introduced later in the game during their respective seasons. You are now ready to begin playing. The player with the purple start player worker goes first in Spring.

bullets: Place 40 blue, 40 red, and 40 yellow workers into the black cloth bag
Place 20 green workers in a visible supply at the side

bullets: Sort 120 resource counters: 48 gold, 24 iron, 24 stone, 24 wood
Place in separate piles as a general supply
Gold substitutes for any resource at any time

Place face down in a single pile at the side

bullets: Each player receives one home screen
Assemble with chimney pieces to lock walls and roofs

bullets: Each player draws 8 workers secretly from the bag

Place behind your screen

12 each of spring, summer, autumn, and winter tiles

Lowest numbered home tile player gets the purple start player worker
Unused home tiles go back in the box

summary.title: Place the Boat Tiles

Place in a row at the edge with the correct player-count side face up
Place indicated workers from bag and skill tiles on/near each boat

summary.title: Place Turn Order Tiles

Place next to boats, closer to center, correct side face up
These determine boat selection order and start player

summary.title: Lay Out Spring Tiles
bullets: 2P: 6, 3P: 7, 4P: 8, 5P: 9, 6P: 10 spring tiles
Place in center with spring icon face up
Surplus tiles go back in the box

summary.title: Deal Winter Tiles
bullets: 2-4 players: 3 winter tiles each; 5-6 players: 2 each
Look at the scoring side secretly, do not reveal
These determine end-game scoring opportunities

summary.title: Set Aside Summer and Autumn Tiles
bullets: Place 12 summer tiles and 12 autumn tiles in two separate piles
These are introduced during their respective seasons
Player with purple start player worker begins Spring

walkthrough.title: The Four Seasons
walkthrough.text: Keyflower is played over four rounds, each representing a season: Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter. Each season follows the same basic structure. New tiles appear for auction, workers arrive on boats, and players take turns placing workers to either bid on tiles or use tiles for production. At the end of each season, tiles are distributed, workers from boats are collected, and villages grow. Winter is the final season, after which the game ends and points are tallied.

walkthrough.title: Start of Each Season
walkthrough.text: At the start of Summer and Autumn, new things happen. First, turn the boat tiles over so the current season's icon is visible. Then place new workers from the bag and skill tiles onto the boats as indicated. Next, lay out new village tiles for the current season in the center, using the same player-count chart from setup. In Winter, there are no new workers on boats. Instead, each player secretly selects one or more of their winter tiles, all players simultaneously reveal how many they chose, and the selected tiles are shuffled face down, then flipped and laid out for auction.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Two Choices
walkthrough.text: On your turn you must do exactly one thing: place one or more workers from behind your screen into a single location. You have two choices. First, you can bid for a village tile or turn order tile by placing workers on your side of that tile. Second, you can use a village tile for production by placing workers directly on the tile. If you cannot or do not want to do either, you pass. Play continues clockwise. Importantly, a player who has passed may take further actions later, as long as all other players have not also passed. The season ends only when all players pass in succession.

walkthrough.title: Bidding for Village Tiles
walkthrough.text: To bid for a village tile, place one or more workers on your side of the tile. Here is the critical color rule: all workers used in bidding for a tile, and all workers placed on the tile for production, must be the same color. If someone has already placed red workers around or on a tile, everyone must use red for that tile. If another player has already bid, your bid must use more workers than theirs. There is no maximum bid. Workers come from behind your screen or from a losing bid elsewhere. You cannot voluntarily remove workers from a winning bid.

walkthrough.title: Outbid Workers
walkthrough.text: When you are outbid, your workers are freed up. They must stay together as a group, but you can move them to bid on another tile or use them for production. You may also supplement outbid workers with additional workers from behind your screen or from other losing bids. However, workers can never be moved back behind your screen once placed on the table.

walkthrough.title: Bidding for Turn Order Tiles
walkthrough.text: Turn order tiles work like village tiles for bidding purposes. They determine two things: the order players choose which boat's contents to take, and who becomes the new start player. The tile showing the purple start player icon determines the next season's start player. A player can bid on multiple turn order tiles, but they only collect workers and skill tiles from one boat. In Winter, turn order tile winners also take the tile itself and one boat tile for their village.

walkthrough.title: Production: Using Tiles
walkthrough.text: Instead of bidding, you can use a tile for production. You can use tiles in your own village, in other players' villages, or on tiles currently being auctioned (except in Winter). Place one worker on the tile for the first use in a season. All workers on a tile must be the same color, matching any workers already there or surrounding it. When you use the tile, you immediately receive the benefit shown on it.

walkthrough.title: Repeated Production
walkthrough.text: A tile can be used up to three times per season. The first use costs one worker. The second use costs two additional workers of the same color. The third use costs three more workers. That means a maximum of six workers can be placed on any single tile per season. If you overspend workers on the first use, such as placing two workers instead of one, the second use requires at least three workers, for a total of five, and the tile cannot be used a third time since the six-worker maximum would be exceeded.

walkthrough.title: Where Resources Go
walkthrough.text: When you generate resources from production, where they end up depends on whose village the tile is in. If you use a tile in your own village, the resources are placed directly on that tile. If you use a tile in another player's village or on a tile being auctioned, the resources go to your home tile. Skill tiles always go behind your screen. Workers drawn from the bag or taken from the green supply also go behind your screen.

walkthrough.title: Transport and Upgrading
walkthrough.text: Your home tile and the transport tiles (Farrier, Stable, Wainwright) allow you to move resources and upgrade tiles. To use transport, place a worker on one of these tiles following normal production rules. You may then move up to the number of resources shown on the tile's transport icon, and upgrade up to the number of tiles shown by the upgrade icons. Resources must travel along roads connecting adjacent tiles. A transport capacity of two means you can move two resources one tile each, or one resource two tiles along a road.

walkthrough.title: Upgrading Tiles
walkthrough.text: Village tiles, except summer boat tiles and winter tiles, can be upgraded by flipping them to their reverse side. The upgrade cost is shown in the upgrade box on the tile. The required resources must already be on the tile when you upgrade it. Those resources are returned to the general supply. Any skill tiles needed for the upgrade come from behind your screen. After flipping, the tile keeps its orientation, and any remaining resources and workers stay on it. Upgraded tiles often provide better benefits and unlock victory points.

walkthrough.title: End of Season
walkthrough.text: The season ends when all players pass in succession. Then several things happen in order. First, players collect workers from unsuccessful bids and place them behind their screens. Second, any village tiles that nobody bid on are removed to the box, and any workers on those tiles go into the bag. Third, starting with the start player and going clockwise, each player takes their won tiles and any workers on them, and places the workers used in the winning bid into the bag.

walkthrough.title: Collecting Home Village Workers
walkthrough.text: After distributing won tiles, all workers that were used on tiles in your own home village are collected and placed back behind your screen. This is important: workers used in your village during the season come back to you, while workers used in other players' villages or on auction tiles are lost to winning bids. In Winter, workers collected this way are available for end-game scoring purposes.

walkthrough.title: Resolving Turn Order Tiles
walkthrough.text: In Spring, Summer, and Autumn, the player who won the number 1 turn order tile picks a boat and takes its workers and skill tiles, placing them behind their screen. Then the number 2 winner picks, then number 3, and so on. After all turn order tile winners have chosen, remaining players pick boats in clockwise order from the start player. The player who won the tile showing the purple start player becomes the new start player. If nobody bid on the start player tile, it passes to the left. Workers used to bid on turn order tiles go into the bag.

walkthrough.title: Winter Turn Order Resolution
walkthrough.text: Winter works differently. There are no new workers on boats. The player who won the number 1 turn order tile takes that tile and picks one boat tile to add to their village. Then the number 2 winner does the same, and so on. Players who won multiple turn order tiles take all of them but only one boat. After turn order winners, remaining players pick boats in clockwise order from the start player. Turn order tiles and boat tiles are all added to players' villages.

walkthrough.title: Expanding Your Village
walkthrough.text: Players simultaneously add their newly won tiles to their village. Tiles are hexagonal and the sides must match when placed adjacent to each other: roads must connect to roads, empty sides to empty sides, and water to water. Spring tiles have four roads, most summer tiles have three, autumn tiles have two, and winter tiles have one. Plan your layout carefully because once a tile is placed, it cannot be repositioned. Resources can only be transported along connected roads, so keep resource-producing tiles near tiles that need resources for upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the Winter season resolves. Players score points from several sources. First, points printed on upgraded (flipped) tiles. Second, points from winter scoring tiles based on your resources, skills, and workers. Third, points from boat tiles based on their specific conditions. Fourth, points from turn order tiles equal to one per adjacent tile. Fifth, gold counters are worth one point each if not allocated to a scoring tile. The purple start player marker can be declared as any resource, skill, or worker color for scoring. The player with the most points wins.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Rules and Restrictions
walkthrough.text: During scoring, each item can only be scored once and allocated to one bonus tile. Resources on the Barn, Blacksmith, Stone Yard, and Timber Yard that were placed during the game stay on those tiles and score as indicated. Those resources cannot also count toward the Jeweller, Mercer's Guild, Watermill, or Windmill. All other resources, skills, and workers can be freely allocated to winter scoring tiles without needing transport. For scoring tiles like the Apothecary and Craftsman's Guild, bonuses can be scored multiple times if you have enough qualifying items. The Keythedral is the exception, scoring a flat 12 points.

summary.title: The Four Seasons
bullets: Game = 4 rounds: Spring, Summer, Autumn, Winter
Each season: new tiles appear, players bid and produce, villages expand
Game ends after Winter, then final scoring

summary.title: Start of Each Season
bullets: Summer/Autumn: flip boats, add new workers and skill tiles, lay out season tiles
Winter: players secretly select winter tiles, shuffle and reveal for auction
No new workers arrive on boats in Winter

summary.title: Your Turn: Two Choices
bullets: 1. Bid for a village tile or turn order tile
2. Use a village tile for production
Or pass — but you may act again if not all players passed in succession
Season ends only when all players pass consecutively

summary.title: Bidding for Village Tiles
bullets: Place workers on your side of the tile to bid
ALL workers on and around a tile must be the same color
Must bid more workers than the current highest bid
Workers come from behind your screen or from outbid groups

summary.title: Outbid Workers
bullets: Outbid workers must stay together as a group
Can be moved to bid elsewhere or used for production
May supplement with workers from screen or other losing bids
Workers can never return behind your screen once placed

summary.title: Bidding for Turn Order Tiles
bullets: Determines boat selection order and next start player
Can bid on multiple turn order tiles, but only pick one boat
In Winter, winners also take the turn order tile and a boat tile for their village

summary.title: Production: Using Tiles
bullets: Place worker(s) on any tile: yours, opponents', or auctioned (not Winter)
Workers must match color of all other workers on/around the tile
Immediately receive the tile's benefit

summary.title: Repeated Production
bullets: 1st use: 1 worker, 2nd use: 2 more, 3rd use: 3 more (max 6 per tile per season)
Each use requires at least one more worker than the previous use
Overspending workers (e.g., 2 on first use) raises costs for subsequent uses

summary.title: Where Resources Go
bullets: Own village: resources placed on the generating tile
Other player's village or auction tile: resources go to your home tile
Skills and new workers always go behind your screen

summary.title: Transport and Upgrading
bullets: Home tile, Farrier, Stable, Wainwright enable transport and upgrades
Move resources along connected roads up to the transport capacity
Transport capacity can be split among multiple resources/directions

summary.title: Upgrading Tiles
bullets: Flip a village tile to its reverse side to upgrade
Required resources must already be on the tile
Resources spent are returned to supply; skill tiles from behind screen
Summer boat tiles and winter tiles cannot be upgraded

summary.title: End of Season
bullets: Collect workers from unsuccessful bids (back behind screen)
Remove unclaimed village tiles to box; workers on them go to bag
Distribute won tiles; winning bid workers go into the bag

summary.title: Collecting Home Village Workers
bullets: Workers used on tiles in your own village return behind your screen
Workers used in other villages are lost to winning bids
In Winter, returned workers count for end-game scoring

summary.title: Resolving Turn Order Tiles
bullets: Spring/Summer/Autumn: turn order winners pick boats in order (1, 2, 3...)
Remaining players pick in clockwise order from start player
Purple start player tile winner becomes new start player
Turn order bid workers go into the bag

summary.title: Winter Turn Order Resolution
bullets: No new workers on boats in Winter
Turn order winners take their tile + one boat tile for their village
Multiple turn order wins = multiple tiles but only one boat
Remaining players pick boats in clockwise order

summary.title: Expanding Your Village
bullets: Hexagonal tile sides must match: road-to-road, field-to-field, water-to-water
Spring: 4 roads, Summer: 3, Autumn: 2, Winter: 1
Tiles cannot be repositioned once placed
Plan roads for resource transport and upgrade accessibility

summary.title: Game End and Final Scoring
bullets: Upgraded tile points + winter scoring tiles + boat points + turn order adjacency
Gold = 1 point each if not allocated to a scoring tile
Purple start player marker counts as any resource, skill, or worker color

summary.title: Scoring Rules and Restrictions
bullets: Each item (resource, skill, worker) can only be scored once
Barn/Blacksmith/Stone Yard/Timber Yard resources are locked to those tiles
All other items freely allocated to winter tiles (no transport needed)
Most winter tiles can score multiple times with enough qualifying items

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Keyflower
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first season, Spring, with four players. Each player has a home tile, a screen hiding eight random workers, and can see the spring tiles laid out for auction, the boat tiles loaded with workers and skill tiles, and the turn order tiles. Follow along move by move to see how bidding, production, and the color rule work in practice.

walkthrough.title: The Table at a Glance
walkthrough.text: The center of the table has eight spring village tiles available for auction. There are three turn order tiles next to them. Beyond those are four boat tiles, each loaded with workers and a skill tile. Every player has a home tile in front of their screen. Player 1 has the purple start player marker because they received the lowest-numbered home tile. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Opening Bid
walkthrough.text: Player 1 looks behind their screen and sees three blue, three red, and two yellow workers. They want the Keywood tile, which generates wood resources. Player 1 places one blue worker on their side of the Keywood tile. That is a bid of one. The Keywood tile is now locked to blue. Anyone who wants to bid on it or use it must also use blue workers.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Bidding on a Different Tile
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has four red workers and four yellow. They want the Apprentice Hall, which gives skill tiles. Player 2 places one red worker on their side of the Apprentice Hall. Now both Keywood and Apprentice Hall have opening bids.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Using a Tile for Production
walkthrough.text: Player 3 decides not to bid yet. Instead, they want to generate resources right away. Player 3 places one yellow worker directly on the Gold Mine tile in the center, which is currently up for auction. This is production, not a bid. Player 3 immediately receives a gold resource from the supply, which goes to their home tile because the Gold Mine is not yet in their village. Now the Gold Mine is locked to yellow for both bidding and production this season.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: The First Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 4 also wants the Keywood tile. They have plenty of workers, including several red ones. Player 4 places two red workers on their side of Keywood to outbid Player 1. But wait! Player 1's bid was with blue workers. All workers on and around a tile must be the same color. Player 4 cannot use red. They must use blue workers to bid on Keywood. This is the single most common mistake in Keyflower. Take those red workers back and try again.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Color Rule
walkthrough.text: The color rule is the heart of Keyflower. Once any worker of a particular color is placed on or around a tile, whether for bidding or production, that color is locked in for the entire season. Every subsequent worker placed on or around that tile must match. This means the colors you have behind your screen dictate what you can compete for. If you have no blue workers and a tile is locked to blue, you are completely shut out. Always check the color before placing workers.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 Corrects the Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 4 checks behind their screen. They do have two blue workers. They place both blue workers on their side of Keywood, outbidding Player 1's single blue. Player 1's blue worker is now outbid. Player 1 will get that worker back when it is their turn, but cannot return it behind the screen. They must use it to bid or produce elsewhere.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Responding to Being Outbid
walkthrough.text: Player 1 really wants Keywood. They take their outbid blue worker and add two more blue workers from behind their screen, placing all three blue on their side of Keywood. That is a bid of three, outbidding Player 4's two. Player 4's two blue workers are now freed. Notice how this bidding war is consuming blue workers quickly. Both players need to consider whether the tile is worth this investment.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Using Another Player's Tile
walkthrough.text: Player 2 wants to generate resources. They see that Player 3 has a home tile. Player 2 can use Player 3's home tile for transport and upgrading. Player 2 places one yellow worker on Player 3's home tile. Player 2 can now move resources in their own village. But there is nothing to transport yet since it is Spring and Player 2 has no village tiles. This was a wasted move.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Waste Workers on Empty Transport
walkthrough.text: Using a transport tile when you have no resources to move and nothing to upgrade is a waste of a worker. In Spring, you rarely need transport because you have not built a village yet. Save transport actions for later seasons when you have resources sitting on tiles that need to be moved to upgrade targets. Workers are your most precious resource in Keyflower. Never waste them.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Bidding for Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Player 3 places one red worker on their side of the number 1 turn order tile. Winning this lets Player 3 pick which boat to take workers from first. The number 1 turn order tile also shows the purple start player icon, so winning it means Player 3 becomes the start player next season.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: The Second Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 4 decides to bid on the Gold Mine. They already spent two blue workers on the losing Keywood bid, and those two freed blue workers must stay together. Player 4 places both freed blue workers on their side of the Gold Mine. But the Gold Mine was locked to yellow by Player 3's earlier production. Player 4 cannot bid with blue workers. This is the color rule catching them again. Those blue workers need to go somewhere that is either blue or uncontested.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 Redirects
walkthrough.text: Player 4 takes back the blue workers and instead places them on an unclaimed tile, the Stable, which has no workers on or around it yet. By placing blue workers, Player 4 locks the Stable to blue. Now only blue workers can be used to bid on or use the Stable this season. Player 4 has a bid of two on the Stable.

walkthrough.title: Continuing the Round
walkthrough.text: Play continues clockwise with each player taking one action per turn. Players bid on tiles they want, use tiles for production, and pass when they have no more profitable moves. Remember, passing is not permanent. If another player acts after you pass, you can take another turn. The season ends only when all players pass one after another without anyone acting in between.

walkthrough.title: End of Spring: Resolving Bids
walkthrough.text: All players have passed. Spring is over. First, collect any workers from unsuccessful bids. Player 4's freed blue workers from the Keywood battle go behind their screen. Next, remove any village tiles nobody bid on, returning their workers to the bag. Then, starting with Player 1, each winner takes their tile and any production workers on it, and places their winning bid workers into the bag. Player 1 wins Keywood with three blue. Those three blue go into the bag. Player 1 adds Keywood to their village.

walkthrough.title: Collecting Boats and Workers
walkthrough.text: Player 3 won the number 1 turn order tile with one red. That red worker goes into the bag. Player 3 picks their preferred boat and takes all the workers and skill tiles from it, placing them behind their screen. Player 3 also takes the purple start player marker. Remaining players who did not win turn order tiles pick boats in clockwise order from the new start player.

walkthrough.title: Building Your Village
walkthrough.text: All players simultaneously add their new tiles to their villages. Remember: adjacent tile sides must match. Roads connect to roads, empty edges to empty edges, water to water. Think about where you will need resources later. Place resource-generating tiles near tiles you plan to upgrade. Once placed, tiles never move. Player 1 places Keywood connected to their home tile by road so they can transport wood later.

walkthrough.title: Preparing for Summer
walkthrough.text: Summer is about to begin. Flip the boat tiles to show the Summer icon. Place new workers from the bag and skill tiles on the boats. Lay out summer village tiles in the center. Player 3 is now the start player. Players have new workers from the boats, plus any workers they recovered from their own villages. The cycle begins again: bid or produce, manage your colors, grow your village.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have played through a full Spring season. You have seen bidding wars, the color rule in action, production on auction tiles, turn order resolution, and village placement. The key lessons: always check the tile's color before placing workers, do not waste workers on transport when you have nothing to move, and keep track of which colors you have. You are ready to continue into Summer.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for beginner strategy on bidding, worker management, and village layout.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every tile type, worker color rule, and season mechanic.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Welcome to Keyflower
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 players through the first season (Spring)
Covers bidding, production, the color rule, and end-of-season resolution
Follow along even if you have a different player count

summary.title: The Table at a Glance
bullets: 8 spring tiles in the center for auction
3 turn order tiles and 4 boat tiles with workers
Each player has a home tile and 8 secret workers behind their screen

summary.title: Player 1: Opening Bid
bullets: Player 1 places 1 blue worker on their side of Keywood
Keywood is now locked to blue for the season
Anyone bidding or producing on Keywood must use blue

summary.title: Player 2: Bidding on a Different Tile
bullets: Player 2 places 1 red worker on their side of the Apprentice Hall
Now two tiles have opening bids in different colors
Each tile's color is independently locked

summary.title: Player 3: Using a Tile for Production
bullets: Player 3 places 1 yellow worker ON the Gold Mine (production, not bidding)
Receives gold resource immediately, placed on their home tile
Gold Mine is now locked to yellow for both bidding and production

summary.title: Player 4: The First Mistake
bullets: Player 4 tries to bid red on Keywood, which is locked to blue
INVALID: all workers on/around a tile must be the same color
Must take red workers back and choose a valid placement

summary.title: Lesson: The Color Rule
bullets: Once a color is placed on or around a tile, it is locked for the season
Bidding and production workers both count for the color lock
If you lack the right color, you are shut out of that tile entirely

summary.title: Player 4 Corrects the Mistake
bullets: Player 4 places 2 blue workers on Keywood, outbidding Player 1
Player 1's outbid blue worker is freed but cannot return behind screen
Must be used elsewhere for bidding or production

summary.title: Player 1: Responding to Being Outbid
bullets: Player 1 takes outbid worker + 2 more blue from screen = 3 blue on Keywood
Outbids Player 4's 2 blue; Player 4's workers are now freed
Bidding wars consume workers quickly — weigh the cost

summary.title: Player 2: Using Another Player's Tile
bullets: Player 2 places a worker on Player 3's home tile for transport
No resources to move and nothing to upgrade — wasted move
Production on other players' tiles is legal but must be useful

summary.title: Lesson: Don't Waste Workers on Empty Transport
bullets: Transport is useless without resources to move or tiles to upgrade
In Spring, you rarely have a village worth transporting through
Workers are your most precious resource — never waste them

summary.title: Player 3: Bidding for Turn Order
bullets: Player 3 bids 1 red on the #1 turn order tile
Wins first pick of boat contents and the start player marker
Turn order is often undervalued by beginners

summary.title: Player 4: The Second Mistake
bullets: Player 4 tries to bid blue on the Gold Mine, which is locked to yellow
Color rule violation — cannot mix colors on a tile
Must redirect freed workers to an uncontested or blue-locked tile

summary.title: Player 4 Redirects
bullets: Player 4 places 2 blue workers on the unclaimed Stable tile
Locks the Stable to blue and establishes a bid of 2
Unclaimed tiles let you set the color to your advantage

summary.title: Continuing the Round
bullets: Play continues clockwise: bid, produce, or pass
Passing is not permanent — you can act again if someone else does
Season ends only when all players pass in succession

summary.title: End of Spring: Resolving Bids
bullets: Collect workers from losing bids (behind screen)
Remove unclaimed tiles to box; workers on them go to bag
Winners take tiles + production workers; bid workers go to bag

summary.title: Collecting Boats and Workers
bullets: Turn order winners pick boats in sequence (#1 first)
Take workers and skill tiles from chosen boat behind screen
Start player marker passes to the winner of the start player tile

summary.title: Building Your Village
bullets: All players simultaneously add tiles to their villages
Adjacent sides must match: road-to-road, field-to-field, water-to-water
Plan roads for future transport; tiles cannot be repositioned

summary.title: Preparing for Summer
bullets: Flip boats to Summer, add new workers and skill tiles
Lay out summer village tiles for auction
New start player begins; cycle repeats

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You completed a full Spring season
Key lessons: check tile color, don't waste transport, track your colors
Ready to continue into Summer and beyond

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips for beginner bidding and worker management strategy
Reference the Appendix for every tile, color rule, and season mechanic
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Understand Your Starting Workers
walkthrough.text: Your eight starting workers are your entire economy for Spring. Before the first turn, look at what colors you have. If you drew five blue and only one red and two yellow, you are strong in blue and should try to lock tiles you want to blue early. Conversely, you are weak in red and yellow, so avoid bidding wars on tiles locked to those colors. The random draw means every game starts differently. Adapt to what you have, not what you wish you had.

walkthrough.title: Set the Color Early
walkthrough.text: If you spot a tile you really want, bid on it first. Being the first bidder lets you choose the color, which gives you a massive advantage. If you lock a desirable tile to your strongest color, opponents who lack that color are effectively shut out. This is one of the most powerful moves in Keyflower. Conversely, if an opponent has already set a color you are weak in, consider whether the tile is worth pursuing at all.

walkthrough.title: Workers Are Currency — Spend Wisely
walkthrough.text: Every worker you place is gone for the season. Bid workers go into the bag. Production workers in your own village come back, but production workers used in other villages are lost. Do not get into bidding wars over every tile. Let some tiles go. A tile won cheaply with one worker is often more valuable than a tile won expensively with four, because those extra three workers could have been used for production or other bids. Budget your workers across the season.

walkthrough.title: Use Other Players' Villages Strategically
walkthrough.text: You can place workers on tiles in other players' villages to generate resources. This is powerful but has a cost. The worker you place stays on their tile and eventually goes into the bag. Also, the resources you generate go to your home tile, not directly where you might need them. Use this tactic when you need a specific resource you cannot generate yourself, or to lock an opponent's tile to a color that benefits you and limits them.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Village Layout
walkthrough.text: When placing tiles, think about roads. Resources can only be transported along connected roads. If you place a resource-generating tile with no road connection to the tile you want to upgrade, you cannot move resources there. Place tiles that produce resources adjacent to or along the same road as tiles that consume resources. Also leave room for future tile placement. A dead end with no matching edges blocks expansion.

walkthrough.title: Win At Least One Turn Order Tile Per Season
walkthrough.text: Turn order tiles are often undervalued by beginners. Winning the number one turn order tile gives you first pick of the boats, which can mean four or five workers and a skill tile. That is a huge injection of resources. Even the lower-numbered tiles give you priority over players who did not bid at all. Additionally, the start player tile gives you the powerful advantage of acting first next season. Invest in turn order, especially in Spring when the worker supply matters most.

walkthrough.title: Study Your Winter Tiles Early
walkthrough.text: You receive your winter tiles during setup, and you should look at them immediately. They tell you what scoring opportunities will be available to you at the end of the game. If you have the Craftsman's Guild, which scores for sets of blue, red, and yellow workers, you should be collecting diverse worker colors all game. If you have the Watermill, which scores for one type of resource, you should stockpile that resource. Your winter tiles should guide every decision from Spring onward.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Overbid in Spring
walkthrough.text: Spring is the most worker-constrained season. You have only eight workers and there are many attractive tiles. It is tempting to bid heavily on that perfect tile, but spending four or five workers on a single Spring tile leaves you with almost nothing for production, turn order, and other bids. In Spring, aim to win one or two tiles cheaply and use remaining workers for production and turn order. The boats will replenish your workers for Summer, when you can be more aggressive.

summary.title: Understand Your Starting Workers
bullets: Check your 8 random workers before your first turn
Your strongest color = your best bidding leverage
Avoid contesting tiles locked to a color you lack
Every game starts differently — adapt to what you draw

summary.title: Set the Color Early
bullets: Being the first bidder lets you choose the color
Lock desirable tiles to your strongest color to shut opponents out
If an opponent locked a tile to your weak color, consider walking away

summary.title: Workers Are Currency — Spend Wisely
bullets: Bid workers go into the bag; production workers in your village return
A tile won with 1 worker beats a tile won with 4 in efficiency
Budget workers across all actions: bidding, production, turn order

summary.title: Use Other Players' Villages Strategically
bullets: You can produce on opponents' tiles, but the worker is lost
Resources generated go to your home tile, not the producing tile
Use this to lock opponent tiles to a color that limits them

summary.title: Plan Your Village Layout
bullets: Resources travel along connected roads only
Place resource producers near upgrade targets
Leave room for future tile placement with matching edges

summary.title: Win At Least One Turn Order Tile Per Season
bullets: #1 turn order = first pick of boat contents (4-5 workers + skill tile)
Start player tile gives first action advantage next season
Invest in turn order, especially in Spring

summary.title: Study Your Winter Tiles Early
bullets: Winter tiles define your end-game scoring strategy
Craftsman's Guild = diverse worker colors; Watermill = one resource type
Let winter tiles guide every decision from Spring onward

summary.title: Do Not Overbid in Spring
bullets: Only 8 workers in Spring — the most constrained season
Win 1-2 tiles cheaply, save workers for production and turn order
Boats replenish workers for Summer, when you can be more aggressive

walkthrough.title: Worker Color Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat the color rule as a weapon. When you place a worker on an opponent's tile for production, you lock that tile to a color. If your opponent lacks that color, they cannot use their own tile for the rest of the season. For example, placing a green worker on an opponent's Carpenter tile locks it to green. Green workers are scarce and hard to acquire, so your opponent likely cannot use their own Carpenter until next season. This is a powerful denial tactic, especially in Autumn and Winter when actions are most critical.

walkthrough.title: Green Worker Scarcity
walkthrough.text: Green workers come from a limited supply of 20 and can only be acquired through specific tiles like the Fair, Peddler, and Store. Green workers are powerful precisely because they are scarce. Using green workers to lock tiles creates situations where opponents simply cannot respond. However, green workers go into the bag when used in bids, diluting the bag with a color nobody asked for. This means green workers used in production are often more valuable than green workers used for bidding, since they come back to you at end of season if used in your own village.

walkthrough.title: Denying Key Tiles
walkthrough.text: Some tiles are disproportionately valuable based on the winter scoring tiles in the game. If you can see that the Craftsman's Guild is in play, tiles that generate diverse worker colors become premium targets. If the Jeweller is visible, gold production tiles spike in value. Watch what tiles opponents are bidding on and what resources they are collecting. Sometimes it is worth outbidding an opponent for a tile you do not even need, just to deny them a critical scoring combo. This is especially important in Autumn when the last production tiles appear.

walkthrough.title: Transport Chain Optimization
walkthrough.text: The Farrier, Stable, and Wainwright tiles have different transport capacities and upgrade abilities. The Wainwright is the most powerful with the highest transport capacity. Build your village so that a single transport action can move resources across multiple tiles to reach an upgrade target. An optimal layout creates a central transport hub connected by roads to both resource producers and upgrade targets. Upgraded tiles like the Farrier and Stable themselves have increased transport capacity, creating a snowball effect.

walkthrough.title: Winter Tile Selection Strategy
walkthrough.text: In Winter, you choose which of your winter tiles to contribute to the auction. This is a critical decision. If you have a tile that perfectly complements your strategy, play it. But also consider what tiles will help your opponents. If you hold a tile that an opponent desperately needs, keeping it out of the game can be devastating. In games with 2-4 players, you have 3 winter tiles and must play at least 1. Playing all 3 gives the most options but also benefits opponents. Playing just 1 keeps two scoring opportunities out of the game entirely.

walkthrough.title: The Barn, Blacksmith, Stone Yard, and Timber Yard Lock
walkthrough.text: These four autumn tiles score points for resources stored on them at game end, but those resources are locked. They cannot also be counted toward the Jeweller, Mercer's Guild, Watermill, or Windmill. This creates a key strategic tension. If you store three iron on the Blacksmith for 6 points, those iron cannot count toward the Mercer's Guild sets. Plan which scoring tiles you will pursue before you start stockpiling resources. Sometimes it is better to skip the storage tiles entirely and keep resources flexible for winter scoring.

walkthrough.title: Summer Boat Tile Power
walkthrough.text: The four summer boat tiles provide unique ongoing abilities that require no workers to activate. Boat 2a lets you ignore matching edges when placing tiles and transport across fields, massively increasing village flexibility. Boat 2b doubles all your transport capacity and upgrade ability, and doubles Keyflower boat scoring. Boat 4a lets you counter-bid with a different color than the established one. Boat 4b lets you ignore the color rule for production. Winning the right summer boat can define your entire strategy. Bid aggressively for these in Summer.

walkthrough.title: Boat Tile Scoring in Winter
walkthrough.text: Each boat tile scores differently. The Keyflower boat scores one point per transport capacity you have, which can be doubled by Boat 2b. The Sea Bastion scores for tiles connected in a road loop. The Sea Breese scores for boats connected by water to your home tile, so leave your home tile's water side accessible. The Flipper lets you upgrade one tile for free and scores 2 points. Plan your village layout around the boat you expect to receive. In Winter, turn order winners pick boats first, so winning turn order is critical for securing the best boat.

walkthrough.title: Worker Collection Engine
walkthrough.text: The Alehouse, Brewer, Inn, and Tavern tiles all generate workers from the bag. Building an engine around these tiles lets you replenish workers throughout the game. The Brewer is particularly strong because it requires giving up a skill tile but draws multiple workers. The Tavern requires discarding a worker but draws more back. Combined with the Hiring Fair for skill tile generation, you can create a cycle that keeps your worker supply high while opponents run low. More workers means more actions per season.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final scoring, allocate resources and workers optimally. The Keythedral scores a flat 12 points and is the single highest-scoring tile. The Key Guild scores 10 points per 5 skill tiles. The Scribes score 10 points per set of 3 different skill types. These are massive point swings. The Apothecary scores 3 points per 5 workers. The Craftsman's Guild scores 3 points per set of blue, red, and yellow. The Village Hall scores 1 per worker of a chosen color. Remember: workers played into your own village in Winter count for scoring, so place workers strategically in the final season even if you do not need the production benefit.

summary.title: Worker Color Manipulation
bullets: Lock opponents' tiles to a color they lack via production
Green workers are ideal for locking — opponents rarely have green
Especially powerful in Autumn and Winter when actions are critical

summary.title: Green Worker Scarcity
bullets: Only 20 green in supply; acquired via Fair, Peddler, Store
Green in production = denial tool; green in bids = dilutes the bag
Green workers used in your own village return to you end of season

summary.title: Denying Key Tiles
bullets: Outbid opponents for tiles critical to their scoring combos
Watch visible winter tiles to predict opponents' strategies
Autumn denial is most impactful — last production tiles appear

summary.title: Transport Chain Optimization
bullets: Wainwright has highest transport; Farrier and Stable are cheaper
Build a central hub connected by roads to producers and upgrade targets
Upgraded transport tiles snowball capacity for future seasons

summary.title: Winter Tile Selection Strategy
bullets: Choose which winter tiles to contribute to the auction
Withhold tiles that help opponents to deny scoring combos
Playing more tiles = more options for everyone, including you
In 2-4 player games: 3 tiles, must play at least 1

summary.title: The Resource Storage Lock
bullets: Barn/Blacksmith/Stone Yard/Timber Yard lock resources for scoring
Locked resources cannot also count for Jeweller, Mercer's, Watermill, Windmill
Plan scoring tile priorities before stockpiling
Sometimes skipping storage tiles keeps resources flexible

summary.title: Summer Boat Tile Power
bullets: Boat 2a: ignore tile edge matching + transport across fields
Boat 2b: double transport, upgrade capacity, and Keyflower boat scoring
Boat 4a: counter-bid with a different color than the locked one
Boat 4b: ignore the color rule for production

summary.title: Boat Tile Scoring in Winter
bullets: Keyflower: 1 pt per transport capacity (doubled by Boat 2b)
Sea Bastion: tiles in a road loop; Sea Breese: boats connected by water
Flipper: free upgrade + 2 pts; Ianvincible: flat 5 pts
Win turn order in Winter to pick the best boat first

summary.title: Worker Collection Engine
bullets: Alehouse, Brewer, Inn, Tavern all draw workers from the bag
Brewer: trade 1 skill tile for multiple workers
Tavern: discard 1 worker, draw more back (net positive)
More workers = more actions per season than opponents

summary.title: End-Game Point Maximization
bullets: Keythedral = 12 pts flat; Key Guild = 10 pts per 5 skills
Scribes = 10 pts per set of 3 different skills; Apothecary = 3 pts per 5 workers
Workers played in your own village in Winter count for scoring
Allocate each item to maximize total points — each scores only once

entries.definition: The wooden meeple-shaped pieces used for bidding and production. There are four colors: blue, red, yellow, and green. Players draw random workers from the bag. Workers are the primary currency of the game.

One of the three common worker colors in the bag (40 total). Blue workers are drawn randomly during setup and from boats. They follow the standard color rule: all workers on and around a tile must be the same color.
One of the three common worker colors in the bag (40 total). Red workers function identically to blue and yellow. The random draw determines your color distribution each game.
One of the three common worker colors in the bag (40 total). Yellow workers function identically to blue and red. Having a majority in one color is a strategic advantage for locking tiles.

A scarce worker color (20 total) that starts outside the bag in a visible supply. Green workers are acquired through specific tiles (Fair, Peddler, Store) and from some boats. Green goes into the bag when used in winning bids, making them useful for color denial.

A special waving purple worker that indicates the start player. Awarded to the lowest-numbered home tile in setup, then to the winner of the start player turn order tile each season. For end-game scoring, it can be declared as any resource, skill, or worker color.

The core rule of Keyflower: all workers placed on a tile (for production) and around a tile (for bidding) must be the same color. Once a color is established on a tile, it is locked for the entire season. This applies to both bidding and production workers.

Placing one or more workers on your side of a tile to win it for your village. You must bid more workers than any current bid. All bid workers must match the tile's established color. Winning bid workers go into the bag at end of season.

Placing workers directly on a tile to receive its benefit. Can be done on your own tiles, opponents' tiles, and tiles being auctioned (except in Winter). First use costs 1 worker; second costs 2 more; third costs 3 more. Maximum 6 workers per tile per season.

If you cannot or do not wish to place workers, you pass. Passing is not permanent. You may act again if another player takes an action after you pass. The season ends only when all players pass in succession without anyone acting in between.

Moving resources along connected roads between adjacent tiles in your village. Transport is enabled by home tiles and transport tiles (Farrier, Stable, Wainwright). The number of resources you can move is shown by the cart icon. Resources cannot cross fields or water unless you have Boat 2a.

Flipping a village tile to its reverse side by paying the indicated cost in resources and/or skill tiles. Resources must already be on the tile. Skill tiles come from behind your screen. Upgraded tiles usually have better production benefits and score victory points. Summer boat tiles and winter tiles cannot be upgraded.

Yellow octagonal counter (48 total). Gold can substitute for any other resource at any time, including during scoring and upgrading. Unallocated gold is worth 1 victory point each at game end. If gold is allocated to a scoring tile, it does not also score as gold.
Black octagonal counter (24 total). Generated by tiles like Key Mine and Miner. Used for upgrading certain tiles and scored by the Blacksmith and various winter tiles.
Grey octagonal counter (24 total). Generated by tiles like Keystone Quarry and Quarryman. Used for upgrading certain tiles and scored by the Stone Yard and various winter tiles.
Brown octagonal counter (24 total). Generated by tiles like Keywood and Woodcutter. Used for upgrading certain tiles and scored by the Timber Yard and various winter tiles.

Small tokens representing crafting skills: anvil, pick axe, or saw (48 total, 16 each). Drawn randomly from a face-down pile. Required for some tile upgrades. Scored by the Scholar, Scribes, and Key Guild winter tiles. Kept hidden behind your screen.

One of three skill tile types (16 total). Represents metalworking skill. Used by the Smelter for production and required for certain upgrades. Scored by the Scholar (3 pts per matching type) and Scribes (10 pts per set of 3 different skills).
One of three skill tile types (16 total). Represents mining skill. Used by the Mason for production and required for certain upgrades. Scored by the Scholar and Scribes.
One of three skill tile types (16 total). Represents woodworking skill. Used by the Carpenter for production and required for certain upgrades. Scored by the Scholar and Scribes.

The first of four seasons. Spring tiles have four roads each, making them the most connectable. Players start with 8 workers and no village tiles. Spring is the most worker-constrained season. The lowest home tile number determines the start player.

The second season. New workers and skill tiles arrive on boats. Summer tiles (8 non-boat) have three roads each. Four special summer boat tiles may appear, which provide unique ongoing abilities with no worker cost. Summer boat tiles have a season icon on both sides and cannot be upgraded.

The third season. Last season with new workers on boats. Autumn tiles have two roads each. Important scoring infrastructure tiles appear (Barn, Blacksmith, Stone Yard, Timber Yard). This is the last chance to acquire production and transport tiles before Winter.

The final season. No new workers arrive on boats. Players secretly select which winter tiles to contribute for auction. Winter tiles have one road each and provide end-game scoring conditions. Turn order winners take turn order tiles and boat tiles for their village. The game ends after Winter resolves.

Each player's starting tile with five roads and one water side. The home tile enables basic transport and upgrading (move 1 resource, upgrade 1 tile). The lowest numbered home tile determines the first start player. Resources from outside your village go to your home tile.

Tiles determining boat selection priority and start player. In Spring through Autumn, they are resolved and returned. In Winter, winners keep them as village tiles scoring 1 point per adjacent tile. The tile showing the purple worker determines the next start player.

Tiles that hold workers and skill tiles for players to collect each season. In Spring through Autumn, contents are distributed based on turn order. In Winter, the physical boat tiles are taken by players and added to their villages. Each boat has a unique scoring condition for end-game points.

The 48 hexagonal tiles (12 per season) that players bid for and add to their villages. Each tile provides a production benefit when workers are placed on it. Most can be upgraded by flipping to the reverse side. Village tiles form the core of each player's village and scoring engine.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to draw the indicated number of workers from the bag. A straightforward worker-generation tile.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take a random skill tile from the face-down pile. When upgraded, you take more skill tiles per use.
Autumn tile. Scores points at game end for each resource of the indicated type stored on it during the game. Resources on the Barn are locked and cannot count toward other scoring tiles.
Autumn tile. Scores points at game end for each iron resource stored on it during the game. Like the Barn, resources here are locked from other scoring tiles.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and return a skill tile to the pile, then draw the indicated number of workers from the bag. Trades skills for workers.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and return a saw skill tile to the pile, then take the indicated resources from the stock. Converts saw skills into resources.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and put an additional worker of the indicated color into the bag, then take the indicated number of green workers from the supply.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. A transport tile. Place a worker to transport resources along roads and upgrade tiles. Lower capacity than the Stable or Wainwright.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Scores victory points at the end of the game only (no production benefit during the game).
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take gold resources from the stock. Gold is the most flexible resource since it substitutes for any other.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Scores victory points at the end of the game only (no production benefit during the game).
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and return a skill tile of any type to the pile, then take two (or three if upgraded) random skill tiles. Net gain of one (or two) skill tiles.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to draw the indicated number of workers from the bag. Similar to the Alehouse but with different worker counts.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take iron resources from the stock.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take stone resources from the stock.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take wood resources from the stock.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and return a pick axe skill tile to the pile, then take the indicated resources from the stock. Converts pick axe skills into resources.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take iron resources from the stock.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and put an additional worker of the indicated color into the bag, then take the indicated number of green workers from the supply.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take stone resources from the stock.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Scores victory points at the end of the game only (no production benefit during the game).
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Scores victory points at the end of the game only (no production benefit during the game).
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and return an anvil skill tile to the pile, then take the indicated resources from the stock. Converts anvil skills into resources.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. A transport tile. Place a worker to transport resources along roads and upgrade tiles. Medium capacity.
Autumn tile. Scores points at game end for each stone resource stored on it during the game. Resources here are locked from other scoring tiles.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and put an additional worker of the indicated color into the bag, then take the indicated number of green workers from the supply.

Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker and discard an additional worker, then draw the indicated number of workers from the bag. The discarded worker enters the bag after drawing. Net worker gain is positive.
Autumn tile. Scores points at game end for each wood resource stored on it during the game. Resources here are locked from other scoring tiles.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. The strongest transport tile. Place a worker to transport resources along roads and upgrade tiles. Highest capacity.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Scores victory points at the end of the game only (no production benefit during the game).
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take wood resources from the stock.
Spring/Summer/Autumn tile. Place a worker to take one of three indicated resource types from the stock. When upgraded, take all three resources.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 3 points for every 5 workers (including green) they own at game end. Workers played into your own village in Winter count.

Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 3 points for each complete set of blue, red, and yellow workers at game end. Green workers cannot substitute. Workers played into your own village in Winter count.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 2 points per gold resource at game end instead of the usual 1 point. Gold allocated to the Jeweller does not also score as regular gold.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 10 points for every 5 skill tiles they possess at game end.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores a flat 12 victory points. The highest single-tile score in the game. Unlike other winter tiles, it does not scale with resources or workers.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 2 points for each green worker they own at game end.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 5 points for each set of three different resources at game end. Gold can substitute for any resource type but then does not score as gold.
Winter scoring tile. The owner selects one type of skill tile and scores 3 points for each skill tile of that type they possess at game end.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 10 points for each complete set of three different skill tiles (anvil, pick axe, saw) at game end.
Winter scoring tile. The owner selects one worker color and scores 1 point for each worker of that color at game end. Workers played into your own village in Winter count.
Winter scoring tile. The owner selects one resource type and scores 1 point per resource of that type at game end. Choosing gold is pointless since gold already scores 1 point.
Winter scoring tile. The owner scores 5 points for every 5 resources of any type at game end.
Boat tile (6 players). Scores 1 point for each worker the player has at game end. Workers scored by Apothecary, Craftsman's Guild, Key Market, or Village Hall cannot also be scored by White Wind.
Boat tile (2+ players). Scores 1 point per transport capacity the player has across all transport tiles. Can be doubled by Summer Boat 2b.
Boat tile (2+ players). Scores 1 point for each tile connected by roads in a loop that returns to the starting point without retracing any road.
Boat tile (3+ players). Scores points for each boat tile (including summer boats) connected by water to the player's home tile. Points vary per boat connected.
Boat tile (4+ players). Allows the player to flip one village tile for free (no upgrade cost) and scores 2 additional points.
Boat tile (5+ players). Scores a flat 5 victory points. No conditions required.
When taking workers from boats at end of Summer and Autumn, take 2 additional workers from the bag. At end of Winter, take 2 workers from the bag (the only new workers any player receives in Winter).
When taking workers from boats at end of Summer and Autumn, also take 1 green worker from the supply. At end of Winter, take 1 green worker from the supply.

Resources can be transported across fields (no roads needed) as well as along roads. When placing village tiles, the player does not need to match tile sides (roads, fields, water). Does not affect Sea Bastion scoring.
All transport capacity and upgrade ability is doubled. If the player also has the Keyflower boat, its scoring is doubled too.
When upgrading tiles, gold, iron, stone, and wood can be substituted for any other resource type. Provides maximum flexibility for upgrades.
For end-game scoring purposes, gold, iron, stone, and wood can count as any other resource type. Provides maximum flexibility for scoring.

If another player has bid using blue, red, or yellow (not green) workers, the owner may counter-bid using a different color (including green). All counter-bid workers must be the same color and are placed horizontally. If all other bidders leave, the horizontal workers stand up and become the new tile color.

When using tiles for production that have already been bid for or used, the owner does not need to follow the color rule. They may use any color workers, including multiple colors at once. Horizontal workers are placed for non-matching colors. Other players must still follow the original standing color.

A path printed on tile edges that connects adjacent tiles. Resources can only be transported along roads. Spring tiles have 4 roads, most summer tiles have 3, autumn tiles have 2, and winter tiles have 1. Adjacent tile edges must match: road-to-road, field-to-field, water-to-water.

A water-printed tile edge. Must match water-to-water when placing adjacent tiles. Home tiles have one water edge. Boat tiles have water edges. The Sea Breese scores points for boats connected by water to the home tile.

When placing a tile adjacent to another, all touching edges must match: road-to-road, field-to-field, water-to-water. Boat tiles do not need water sides connected. Summer Boat 2a overrides this rule, allowing any edge to touch any other edge.

A foldable screen with chimney pieces that hides your workers, skill tiles, and other secret information. Workers behind the screen can be placed for bidding or production. Once a worker leaves the screen, it cannot return.

The black bag holding blue, red, and yellow workers. Workers are drawn randomly from the bag during setup, for boat contents, and through tile production. Winning bid workers and green workers used in bids are returned to the bag at end of season.
